options.D: Amazon OpenSearch with time-based indices. Index lifecycle management (ILM) for hot/warm/cold tiering. Rollup jobs for downsampling.

explanation: Amazon Timestream is purpose-built for IoT time-series at scale: 1) Memory store provides sub-second queries on recent high-resolution data (automatically ingested here). 2) Magnetic store provides cost-effective storage for historical data (10x cheaper than memory store). 3) Data automatically moves from memory to magnetic based on retention policies (no manual management). 4) Scheduled queries can aggregate/downsample data automatically (e.g., 1-second resolution → 1-minute averages for data older than 7 days). 5) Multi-measure records store multiple metrics per row efficiently (reducing storage and query cost). 6) Built-in time-series functions (interpolation, smoothing, forecasting) optimize analytics. 7) Handles 1M writes/second with serverless scaling. Option B (InfluxDB on EC2) requires managing infrastructure. Option C (DynamoDB) isn't optimized for time-range queries at this scale. Option D (OpenSearch) costs more for pure time-series workloads.

question:
A company's data platform team needs to provide self-service analytics capabilities to 500 business users. Currently, all analytics requests go through the data team (2-week backlog). Business users need to: discover available datasets, understand table schemas and data quality, write and run SQL queries without impacting production workloads, share analyses with colleagues, and schedule recurring reports.

Which combination of services creates a self-service analytics platform?

options.A: Amazon DataZone for data discovery (browse catalog, understand schemas, see quality metrics). Athena for SQL querying (workgroups isolating user queries from production). Athena saved queries and views for sharing. EventBridge Scheduler + Athena named queries for recurring reports. QuickSight for visualization and report distribution.
options.B: Glue Data Catalog accessible via console for discovery. Redshift Serverless for querying. QuickSight for dashboards. Email-based report distribution.
options.C: Custom data portal built on Amplify. Athena backend. No discovery features. Manual schema documentation in Confluence.
options.D: Jupyter Notebooks on SageMaker Studio for all analytics. No SQL access. Python/pandas only. Shared notebook spaces.

explanation: This provides a complete self-service stack: 1) DataZone provides a business-friendly data catalog where users browse, search, understand datasets, see data quality scores, and request access (reduces the 2-week backlog by enabling discovery without the data team). 2) Athena workgroups provide SQL querying with resource isolation (user queries don't impact production ETL). 3) Athena saved queries and views enable knowledge sharing. 4) EventBridge + Athena enables scheduled reports. 5) QuickSight provides visualization for non-SQL users and automated report distribution. Together, these eliminate most of the data team's backlog. Option B is functional but Glue Catalog console isn't user-friendly for business users (no descriptions, quality metrics, or discovery features). Option C requires custom development. Option D excludes SQL users.

question:
A company needs to implement data lineage tracking across their AWS data platform. They need to know: where each dataset originated (source system), what transformations were applied, which downstream datasets depend on a given source (impact analysis), and when data was last refreshed at each stage. The platform uses Glue ETL, EMR Spark, Athena, and Redshift.

Which approach provides the MOST comprehensive automated lineage?

options.A: Manual documentation in a wiki. Data stewards update lineage diagrams after each pipeline change. Quarterly reviews for accuracy.
options.B: AWS Glue Data Catalog metadata with custom properties for lineage. Glue ETL job scripts parsed for source/target information. Custom Lambda extracting lineage from CloudTrail events.
options.C: Amazon DataZone with lineage visualization. OpenLineage integration: Glue ETL jobs emit OpenLineage events automatically (source tables, transformations, target tables). EMR Spark jobs instrumented with OpenLineage Spark listener (automatic SQL parsing). Athena query logs captured via CloudTrail for read lineage. DataZone displays end-to-end lineage graphs with transformation details. CloudWatch metrics for data freshness at each stage (last successful job run time).
options.D: Apache Atlas deployed on EMR for lineage management. Custom hooks in every pipeline script to report lineage. Atlas UI for visualization.

explanation: DataZone with OpenLineage provides automated, comprehensive lineage: 1) OpenLineage is the industry-standard lineage protocol — Glue and Spark both support it natively (no custom code in ETL jobs). 2) Glue ETL automatically emits OpenLineage events capturing which tables are read and written. 3) Spark OpenLineage listener on EMR automatically captures lineage from Spark SQL (parses query plans). 4) CloudTrail captures Athena query execution (read patterns). 5) DataZone aggregates all lineage into a visual graph showing end-to-end data flow. 6) Impact analysis is visual — click any dataset to see all downstream dependencies. 7) CloudWatch tracks freshness (last refresh time at each pipeline stage). Option A (manual) is always stale. Option B (custom parsing) requires ongoing maintenance. Option D (Atlas) requires managing infrastructure and custom hook development.

question:
A company has 500 MySQL databases on Amazon RDS (one per tenant in their SaaS platform). They need to consolidate all tenant data into a single data lake for cross-tenant analytics (with proper tenant isolation). Each database is 10-50 GB. They need ongoing synchronization with less than 1 hour latency. Setting up and maintaining 500 individual DMS tasks is operationally infeasible.

Which approach scales to 500 sources with minimal operational overhead?

options.A: AWS DMS with 500 individual replication tasks. One replication instance per 10 source databases.
options.B: DMS Serverless with multi-task replication instances. Group databases by size into DMS replication tasks (multiple source endpoints per task). DMS Serverless auto-scales compute. S3 target with prefix per tenant_id. Glue ETL for post-processing into Iceberg tables with tenant_id as partition.
options.C: Custom application reading MySQL binlogs from all 500 databases. Publish to Kinesis. Lambda processing to S3.
options.D: Aurora zero-ETL integration for all 500 databases to a single Redshift cluster. Cross-tenant queries in Redshift.

explanation: DMS Serverless reduces the operational burden of 500 sources: 1) Serverless mode automatically scales capacity based on load (no sizing replication instances). 2) Grouping multiple source endpoints into fewer replication tasks reduces management overhead (DMS supports multi-table tasks from different sources). 3) S3 target with tenant-prefixed paths provides logical separation. 4) Glue ETL consolidates into Iceberg tables partitioned by tenant_id (enabling both cross-tenant analytics and tenant-isolated queries). The sub-1-hour latency is achievable with CDC. Option A (500 tasks) is operationally infeasible as stated. Option C (custom binlog reader) is massive engineering effort for 500 databases. Option D (zero-ETL) currently supports one Aurora cluster per Redshift integration, not 500 RDS instances.

question:
A company is building a lakehouse architecture that combines data lake flexibility with data warehouse query performance. They want: open table formats for vendor independence, ACID transactions for reliable updates, sub-second query performance on recent data, the ability to query with both Spark (data engineering) and SQL (analysts), and governance across all data assets.

Which technology combination provides a complete lakehouse on AWS?

options.A: S3 for storage. Apache Iceberg for table format. AWS Glue Data Catalog as metastore. Athena for SQL analytics (reads Iceberg natively). EMR Spark for data engineering (reads/writes Iceberg). Lake Formation for governance. Redshift Spectrum for high-performance joins on Iceberg tables.
options.B: S3 + Delta Lake. Databricks for everything (processing, SQL, governance). No AWS-native service integration.
options.C: Redshift as the lakehouse. All data loaded into Redshift. Redshift's Aqua for acceleration. Redshift ML for data science.
options.D: S3 with Parquet files (no table format). Glue Catalog. Athena for SQL. EMR for Spark. Lake Formation for governance.

explanation: This provides a complete AWS-native lakehouse: 1) S3 for durable, cost-effective storage (open, no lock-in). 2) Apache Iceberg provides ACID transactions, schema evolution, time-travel, and partition evolution (open format — portable across engines). 3) Glue Data Catalog as the Iceberg catalog enables all services to share table metadata. 4) Athena reads Iceberg natively with sub-second performance (SPICE-like acceleration for recent data). 5) EMR Spark reads/writes Iceberg for complex data engineering. 6) Lake Formation provides fine-grained governance (column/row security on Iceberg tables). 7) Redshift Spectrum can join local Redshift data with Iceberg tables for high-performance analytics. Option B (Databricks) creates vendor lock-in. Option C (Redshift only) loses data lake flexibility. Option D (no table format) lacks ACID transactions and efficient updates.

question:
A company runs 300 scheduled Glue ETL jobs. Job failures occur daily due to various reasons: source data format changes, S3 throttling, Glue capacity limits, and data quality issues. The on-call engineer spends hours diagnosing each failure. They need automated failure classification, root cause identification, and resolution guidance.

Which approach MOST effectively reduces mean time to resolve (MTTR) for ETL failures?

options.A: CloudWatch Logs with detailed Glue job logging. CloudWatch Logs Insights queries to search for common error patterns. Manual investigation by on-call engineer.
options.B: Implement standardized error handling in all Glue jobs (try/catch with classified exception types). Failed jobs emit structured error events to EventBridge (including error category: SCHEMA_CHANGE, THROTTLING, CAPACITY, DATA_QUALITY). EventBridge rules route to specific resolution runbooks in Systems Manager Automation (auto-retry for throttling, alert data owner for schema changes, request capacity increase for capacity errors). Glue job metrics in CloudWatch with anomaly detection for early warning. Slack notifications with error classification and direct link to the relevant runbook.
options.C: Deploy Amazon DevOps Guru for Glue monitoring. Rely on ML-based anomaly detection to identify issues. Manual resolution based on DevOps Guru recommendations.
options.D: Implement comprehensive unit tests for all ETL jobs. If a job passes tests, it won't fail in production. Prevention over detection.

explanation: This approach automates the entire incident workflow: 1) Structured error classification in job code means failures are immediately categorized (no human diagnosis needed for common cases). 2) EventBridge routing based on error category triggers the appropriate response automatically (throttling → retry, schema change → alert data owner, capacity → scale request). 3) SSM Automation runbooks can execute resolution steps without human intervention for known failure modes. 4) Anomaly detection provides early warning before failures impact SLAs. 5) Slack notifications with classification and runbook links mean the on-call engineer has immediate context when automation can't resolve. This reduces MTTR from hours to minutes for ~80% of failures. Option A (manual investigation) doesn't reduce MTTR. Option C (DevOps Guru) helps detect but doesn't automate resolution. Option D (unit tests) prevents some failures but not runtime issues like throttling or capacity.

question:
A company's EMR Spark jobs process sensitive data. They need to ensure: Spark executors are isolated (multi-tenant cluster where different teams' jobs shouldn't access each other's data), data in transit between Spark nodes is encrypted, temporary data written to local disks during shuffle operations is encrypted, and access to S3 data is controlled based on the submitting user's permissions.

Which EMR security configuration addresses all requirements?

options.A: EMR security configuration with: at-rest encryption enabled (local disk encryption using LUKS with KMS key for shuffle data), in-transit encryption enabled (TLS certificates for inter-node communication), Kerberos authentication for multi-tenancy (each user gets a Kerberos principal), and Lake Formation integration (EMR uses the submitting user's Lake Formation permissions for S3 access via credential vending). Separate YARN queues per team with resource limits.
options.B: S3 encryption for at-rest data. VPC with security groups for network isolation. IAM roles per application. No local disk encryption.
options.C: EMR on EKS with separate pods per team. Pod-level IAM roles. Network policies for isolation. Encrypted EBS volumes for pod storage.
options.D: Dedicated EMR clusters per team (no multi-tenancy). Each cluster has its own KMS key. VPC peering between clusters.

explanation: EMR security configuration provides all four requirements: 1) LUKS local disk encryption ensures shuffle data (written to local disks during Spark shuffles) is encrypted — this data often contains sensitive intermediate results. 2) TLS in-transit encryption protects data flowing between Spark driver, executors, and HDFS. 3) Kerberos authentication provides multi-tenancy — each user is authenticated and YARN enforces that jobs only see their own data. 4) Lake Formation integration with credential vending passes the submitting user's permissions to Spark executors — each job only accesses S3 data the user is authorized for via Lake Formation (column/row level security applied). YARN queues provide resource isolation. Option B lacks disk encryption and multi-tenancy. Option C (EKS) works but is a different architecture. Option D (separate clusters) wastes resources and doesn't address the multi-tenant requirement.

question:
A company needs to implement a Change Data Capture (CDC) pipeline from their Amazon Aurora PostgreSQL database to their S3 data lake. They need: all INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE operations captured, the ability to distinguish between operation types in the data lake, ordering guarantees within each table, and the ability to reconstruct the table state at any point in time from the CDC log.

Which CDC implementation provides complete event capture with point-in-time reconstruction?

options.A: AWS DMS with CDC mode, writing to S3 in CSV format. Include operation indicator column. Sort by transaction timestamp.
options.B: AWS DMS with CDC mode writing to Kinesis Data Streams. Kinesis delivers to S3 via Firehose in Parquet format. DMS includes: op column (I/U/D for operation type), transaction_commit_timestamp, and full row values (before/after images for updates). S3 organized as append-only CDC log partitioned by date/hour. Apache Iceberg table built from CDC using MERGE INTO (apply CDC events to maintain current-state table). Iceberg time-travel enables point-in-time state reconstruction from the materialized table history.
options.C: Aurora PostgreSQL logical replication to a custom application. Application writes to S3. Manual implementation of ordering and operation tracking.
options.D: Aurora Database Activity Streams to Kinesis. Parse DAS events for data changes. Write to S3.

explanation: This provides a complete CDC solution with reconstruction capability: 1) DMS captures all INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE operations from Aurora's write-ahead log (non-invasive). 2) The op column and before/after images in each CDC record provide complete change information. 3) Kinesis maintains ordering per partition (table). 4) S3 CDC log is append-only (raw CDC events preserved permanently). 5) Iceberg MERGE INTO applies CDC events to maintain a current-state table (inserts→insert, updates→update matching rows, deletes→delete matching rows). 6) Iceberg time-travel enables point-in-time reconstruction — query `SELECT * FROM table FOR TIMESTAMP AS OF '2024-01-15'` returns the table state at that time. Option A (CSV to S3) works for basic CDC but lacks the Iceberg integration for efficient reconstruction. Option C (custom) is over-engineering. Option D (DAS) is for auditing, not data replication.

question:
A company's Redshift cluster frequently runs out of disk space during complex queries (intermediate results exceed available space). The cluster is 8 dc2.8xlarge nodes with 2.56 TB SSD per node (20.5 TB total). The data stored is 15 TB but complex joins generate 10+ TB of intermediate results. Adding more nodes solely for temp space is expensive.

Which solution addresses the disk space issue MOST cost-effectively?

options.A: Add 8 more dc2.8xlarge nodes (double the cluster). Accept the additional $150K/month cost.
options.B: Migrate to RA3 nodes (ra3.4xlarge, 16 nodes). RA3 separates compute from storage — managed storage automatically extends to S3 for overflow. Temp space is also managed and can exceed local SSD capacity. Scale compute independently of storage. Concurrency scaling handles burst query workload.
options.C: Optimize queries to reduce intermediate result sizes. Use SORT MERGE joins instead of HASH joins. Add distribution keys to reduce cross-node shuffling.
options.D: Implement Redshift Spectrum and move most data to S3. Only keep frequently joined data in Redshift. This reduces both stored data and intermediate results.

explanation: RA3 nodes solve the disk space problem architecturally: 1) Managed storage automatically extends beyond local SSD to S3 — both for stored data and for temporary/intermediate results. No more disk full errors. 2) You pay for compute separately from storage (likely cheaper than dc2 at this scale). 3) With 16 ra3.4xlarge nodes you get equivalent compute but virtually unlimited storage. 4) Concurrency scaling handles additional query load without over-provisioning. 5) The migration from dc2 to RA3 is straightforward (resize operation). Option A (double dc2) is the most expensive solution. Option C (query optimization) helps but doesn't solve the fundamental space limitation for legitimately complex queries. Option D (Spectrum) reduces stored data but complex joins still generate large intermediate results locally.

question:
A data lake team needs to implement data cataloging for 10,000 tables across their S3 data lake. They need: automated schema detection for new data, column-level statistics (min, max, null count, distinct count), data profiling (sample values, distribution histograms), business metadata (descriptions, owners, tags), and search functionality for analysts to find relevant tables.

Which implementation provides comprehensive cataloging with minimal manual effort?

options.A: AWS Glue Crawlers on schedule to detect/update schemas. Glue Data Catalog for technical metadata. Column statistics generated by Athena ANALYZE TABLE commands (run periodically). DataZone for business metadata (descriptions, glossary terms, ownership), search, and discovery. Glue Data Quality for profiling. DataZone custom metadata forms for owner/team attribution.
options.B: Apache Hive Metastore on EMR. Manual schema registration. No statistics. Custom web portal for search.
options.C: Glue Crawlers only. Glue Data Catalog with manually added descriptions. No statistics or profiling. Console-based search.
options.D: Custom Python scripts scanning S3, generating schema definitions, computing statistics, and storing results in DynamoDB. Custom search API on Lambda.

explanation: This provides comprehensive cataloging: 1) Glue Crawlers automatically detect schemas for new/changed data (minimal manual effort for 10,000 tables). 2) Athena ANALYZE TABLE computes column statistics (used by query planners for optimization). 3) Glue Data Quality provides data profiling (value distributions, patterns). 4) DataZone adds the business layer — descriptions, glossary, ownership, and powerful search (analysts can discover tables by business context, not just technical names). 5) Custom metadata forms capture structured business metadata (owner, SLA, refresh frequency). The combination provides both technical completeness and business usability. Option B (Hive Metastore) lacks all modern features. Option C (basic Crawlers) misses statistics, profiling, and business metadata. Option D (custom) is significant engineering effort to build and maintain.

question:
A company needs to ensure that their data pipeline infrastructure is deployed securely and compliant with their security standards. Requirements: all S3 buckets must have encryption enabled, all Glue connections must use SSL, all Redshift clusters must be in a VPC with no public accessibility, and all KMS keys must have key rotation enabled. These checks must run continuously and non-compliant resources must be flagged within 15 minutes.

Which approach provides continuous compliance monitoring for data infrastructure?

options.A: Manual security audit quarterly. Checklist-based review of all data resources. Findings reported in a spreadsheet.
options.B: AWS Config rules (managed and custom) for continuous evaluation: s3-bucket-server-side-encryption-enabled, redshift-cluster-public-access-check, custom Config rule checking Glue connection SSL settings, cmk-backing-key-rotation-enabled. Config evaluates on resource change events (detected within minutes). Non-compliant findings reported to Security Hub. Automated remediation via Config remediation actions (e.g., enable S3 encryption automatically). Dashboard in Security Hub showing compliance score.
options.C: AWS Trusted Advisor checks for security. Trusted Advisor refresh interval (weekly). SNS notifications for new findings.
options.D: Lambda function scanning all resources on a daily cron schedule. Custom checks for each requirement. Results stored in DynamoDB. Email report to security team.

explanation: AWS Config provides continuous, event-driven compliance monitoring: 1) Managed rules cover common checks (S3 encryption, Redshift public access, KMS rotation) with no custom code. 2) Custom Config rules handle Glue-specific checks. 3) Evaluation triggers on resource changes (creates, updates) — detecting non-compliance within minutes (meets 15-minute requirement). 4) Security Hub aggregation provides a centralized compliance dashboard with scoring. 5) Automated remediation can fix common issues (enabling encryption) without human intervention. 6) Config records a history of compliance state for audit purposes. Option A (quarterly audit) is too infrequent. Option C (Trusted Advisor) has weekly refresh — too slow. Option D (daily Lambda) misses the 15-minute detection requirement.

question:
A company needs to build a data pipeline that processes data from Amazon MSK (Kafka). The pipeline must: consume from 50 Kafka topics, apply different transformation logic per topic, handle schema evolution (new fields added to messages), maintain exactly-once processing semantics, and write results to different S3 locations per topic.

Which consumer architecture handles the complexity with the LEAST operational overhead?

options.A: AWS Glue streaming ETL jobs consuming from MSK. One Glue streaming job per topic group (5 jobs, each handling 10 topics with conditional logic). Glue Schema Registry for schema evolution (backward compatibility mode). Glue checkpoint for exactly-once via offset tracking. Glue DynamicFrame's flexibility handles evolving schemas without job restarts.
options.B: Lambda with MSK event source mapping. One Lambda function per topic. Custom deserialization with schema handling. DynamoDB for deduplication. S3 for output.
options.C: Self-managed Kafka Connect cluster on EC2. Custom sink connectors per topic. Schema Registry (Confluent). Connect's offset management for delivery guarantees.
options.D: EMR Spark Structured Streaming. Read from all 50 topics. Kafka-specific partition handling. S3 checkpoint for offset tracking.

explanation: Glue streaming ETL is the lowest-overhead solution: 1) Serverless — no infrastructure to manage (vs EC2 Kafka Connect or EMR). 2) Native MSK/Kafka consumer with automatic offset management. 3) Grouping topics into a few jobs (5 jobs × 10 topics) with conditional logic is manageable and efficient. 4) Glue Schema Registry with backward compatibility allows producers to add fields without breaking consumers. 5) DynamicFrames naturally handle schema evolution — new fields are automatically incorporated. 6) Glue checkpoint provides exactly-once semantics (committed offsets after successful writes). 7) Per-topic S3 output paths via conditional routing in the job. Option B (Lambda) has cold start issues and limited exactly-once semantics. Option C (Kafka Connect on EC2) requires cluster management. Option D (EMR) requires cluster management.

question:
A company needs to implement a data warehouse that supports both batch loading (nightly 500 GB loads from the data lake) and real-time streaming ingestion (continuous small writes from application events at 50,000 records/second). Analysts need to query both the batch-loaded historical data and the streaming recent data together in a single query without managing separate systems.

Which Redshift architecture supports unified batch and streaming workloads?

options.A: Two Redshift clusters: one for batch, one for streaming. Cross-database queries to join them. Manual consistency management.
options.B: Amazon Redshift with streaming ingestion from Amazon Kinesis Data Streams. Materialized views defined on the Kinesis stream (auto-refreshing). Batch data loaded via COPY from S3 nightly. Analysts query both in the same SQL context (materialized view + regular tables joined together). Redshift Serverless for auto-scaling with mixed workload patterns.
options.C: Redshift for batch only. Real-time data stays in DynamoDB. Application joins results from both. No unified SQL query possible.
options.D: Redshift with micro-batch loading every 5 minutes from Kinesis via Firehose→S3→COPY. No true streaming — 5-minute latency for recent data.

explanation: Redshift streaming ingestion provides unified batch and real-time: 1) Streaming ingestion directly from Kinesis into Redshift materialized views — data is queryable within seconds of arriving in Kinesis (no intermediate S3 staging). 2) Materialized views auto-refresh from the stream continuously. 3) Batch data loads via COPY for nightly historical data. 4) Analysts write single SQL queries joining materialized views (streaming) with regular tables (batch) — no separate systems or application-level joins. 5) Redshift Serverless scales compute automatically for varying workload patterns. Option A (two clusters) adds complexity and can't provide unified queries easily. Option C splits systems requiring application logic. Option D (5-minute micro-batch) adds latency and S3 staging overhead.

question:
A company's data pipeline processes customer transactions. They need to ensure that the number of records processed matches expected counts at every stage (source → landing → staging → warehouse). When counts diverge, they need to identify exactly where records were gained or lost. This reconciliation must happen automatically after every pipeline run.

Which data reconciliation approach provides the MOST reliable end-to-end validation?

options.A: Manual count comparison in spreadsheets after each run. Data engineers spot-check periodically.
options.B: Embed record counting at each pipeline stage. Glue ETL jobs publish custom CloudWatch metrics: records_read and records_written per stage. Step Functions checks count consistency between stages (reads of stage N should equal writes of stage N-1, accounting for known filters). Discrepancies trigger investigation workflow. A reconciliation DynamoDB table stores expected vs actual counts with variance thresholds. Automated alerting when variance exceeds threshold.
options.C: Count records in the final warehouse table. Compare to source count daily. No intermediate stage tracking.
options.D: AWS Glue Data Quality IsComplete check at final stage only. Pass/fail binary result.

explanation: This provides stage-by-stage reconciliation: 1) Record counts published at every stage (source count, landing count, staging count, warehouse count) create a complete audit trail. 2) Step Functions automatically validates that counts flow correctly between stages (if source has 1000 records and staging has 998, is the 2-record difference from known filters or a bug?). 3) DynamoDB reconciliation table provides historical tracking and variance thresholds (some stages may legitimately filter records — configure expected variance). 4) Automatic alerting means no manual checking needed. 5) Stage-by-stage tracking pinpoints exactly where records are gained/lost. Option A (manual) is error-prone and slow. Option C (source vs final only) can't identify which stage has the problem. Option D (binary pass/fail) doesn't provide count-level granularity.

question:
A data lake needs to support data masking for different user personas. The same customer table should show: full data to data engineers (for pipeline development and debugging), partially masked data to analysts (full name visible, SSN masked as ***-**-1234, email masked as j***@domain.com), and fully anonymized data to data scientists (all PII replaced with consistent fake values for ML training that maintains data distribution).

Which approach provides dynamic masking without maintaining multiple copies of data?

options.A: Create three separate tables (full, masked, anonymized). Sync them via ETL after every update to the source.
options.B: AWS Lake Formation with tag-based access control and dynamic data masking. Define data filters per tag: PII_FULL tag granted to engineer group (no masking), PII_PARTIAL tag granted to analyst group (Lake Formation cell-level filter applying regex masking to SSN/email columns), PII_ANONYMOUS tag granted to data science group (Lake Formation data filter applying hash-based consistent anonymization). Single Iceberg table — masking applied at query time based on the querying user's Lake Formation permissions. No separate tables needed.
options.C: Athena views per persona with CASE statements masking columns. Analysts use the masked view, engineers use the base table.
options.D: Redshift dynamic data masking policies. Create masking policies per column per role. Requires data to be in Redshift.

explanation: Lake Formation dynamic data masking provides exactly this capability: 1) Single source table (no data duplication). 2) Tag-based access determines what masking is applied per user/group at query time. 3) Cell-level security filters can transform column values (mask, hash, anonymize) based on the querying user's grants. 4) Consistent anonymization (hash-based) maintains referential integrity for ML (same customer always gets the same fake value). 5) Works with Athena, EMR, and Redshift Spectrum — any query tool respects the masking rules. 6) No maintenance of multiple table copies. Option A (separate tables) triples storage and requires sync maintenance. Option C (views) can be bypassed by querying the base table directly. Option D (Redshift) only works for data in Redshift, not the broader data lake.

question:
A company processes geospatial data for their mapping platform. They receive: GPS trace files (GPX format, millions of files per day), satellite imagery tiles (GeoTIFF, 10 TB per day), and point-of-interest updates (GeoJSON, 1 million records per day). They need to: convert all formats to a unified geospatial format, index for spatial queries (find all data within a bounding box), and support both batch analytics and real-time spatial queries.

Which architecture handles geospatial data processing on AWS?

options.A: S3 for raw data storage. EMR with Apache Sedona (GeoSpark) for spatial transformations and batch analytics. Spatial UDFs for format conversion (GPX→GeoParquet, GeoTIFF metadata extraction, GeoJSON→GeoParquet). Output as GeoParquet with spatial indexing (Hilbert curve partitioning). Amazon OpenSearch with geo_point/geo_shape mappings for real-time spatial queries. Athena with spatial functions for ad-hoc batch queries on GeoParquet.
options.B: PostGIS on RDS for all geospatial data. Import all formats into PostGIS. Spatial indexes for queries. Scale vertically as needed.
options.C: S3 with GeoJSON storage. Custom Lambda for format conversion. DynamoDB with geohash-based GSI for spatial queries. Athena for batch.
options.D: Amazon Location Service for all geospatial operations. Store data in Location Service's native format. Use Location Service APIs for queries.

explanation: This architecture handles geospatial data at scale: 1) EMR with Apache Sedona provides distributed spatial processing (handles millions of GPX files and 10 TB GeoTIFF in parallel). 2) Sedona's spatial UDFs convert formats efficiently at scale. 3) GeoParquet is the emerging standard for geospatial data on data lakes (column-oriented, spatial-indexed). 4) Hilbert curve partitioning ensures spatial locality (nearby features stored together for efficient bounding box queries). 5) OpenSearch with geo_shape handles real-time spatial queries with sub-second latency. 6) Athena with spatial functions (ST_Contains, ST_Distance, etc.) handles batch analytics on GeoParquet. Option B (PostGIS) can't handle 10 TB/day of imagery. Option C (DynamoDB geohash) has limited spatial query capabilities. Option D (Location Service) is for application features, not bulk data processing.

question:
A company needs to implement a data retention and lifecycle policy for their 500 TB data lake. Requirements: raw data retained for 7 years (regulatory), processed data retained for 3 years, aggregate/summary data retained for 10 years, data must be deletable within 30 days for GDPR erasure requests, and access patterns show 90% of queries hit data from the last 30 days.

Which storage lifecycle strategy MINIMIZES cost while meeting all requirements?

options.A: All data in S3 Standard. Manual deletion after retention period. No tiering.
options.B: S3 Intelligent-Tiering for everything. Let AWS optimize automatically. Lifecycle policy to delete after 10 years.
options.C: Tiered lifecycle per data category: Raw data: S3 Standard (30 days) → S3 Standard-IA (30-365 days) → S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval (1-3 years) → S3 Glacier Deep Archive (3-7 years) → delete. Processed data: S3 Standard (30 days) → S3 Standard-IA (30 days-1 year) → Glacier Instant Retrieval (1-3 years) → delete. Aggregates: S3 Standard-IA always (small size, infrequent access, 10-year retention). Use Apache Iceberg tables for all data (row-level DELETE for GDPR erasure across all storage classes — Iceberg rewrites only affected files). Tag-based lifecycle rules keyed on data_category tag.
options.D: Raw data immediately to Glacier Deep Archive. Processed data to Glacier. Only aggregates in Standard. Accept 12-hour retrieval time for raw data queries.

explanation: This strategy maps access patterns to cost tiers optimally: 1) 90% of queries hit 30-day data (S3 Standard for hot data = fast performance). 2) Older data transitions to progressively cheaper tiers matching declining access frequency. 3) Different retention periods per category implemented via separate lifecycle rules. 4) Iceberg's row-level DELETE enables GDPR erasure — even data in Glacier-class storage can be deleted by rewriting only the affected data files (Iceberg metadata update marks files for deletion). 5) Tag-based rules enable different policies on the same bucket. 6) Glacier Instant Retrieval (millisecond access) vs Deep Archive (hours) chosen based on whether data might need occasional querying. Option A (all Standard) is the most expensive. Option B (Intelligent-Tiering for everything) doesn't respect the different retention periods. Option D (immediate Glacier) makes raw data too slow for the rare queries that do access it.

question:
A company wants to implement automated testing for their Glue ETL jobs before deploying to production. Their jobs read from S3, apply transformations, and write to Iceberg tables. They need to test: transformation logic correctness, handling of edge cases (nulls, malformed data, schema drift), performance regression detection, and data quality rule compliance.

Which testing strategy provides comprehensive ETL test coverage?

options.A: Manual testing in a dev environment. Visual inspection of output data. Approval by data engineer before deployment.
options.B: Unit tests using pytest with local Spark (moto for S3 mocking). Sample input fixtures representing normal and edge cases. Assert output schema and values match expected. Integration tests in a dev Glue environment using small representative datasets. Performance tests comparing job duration against baseline (fail if 2x slower). Data quality assertions using Glue Data Quality DQDL rules embedded in test jobs. All tests run in CodePipeline before deployment to production.
options.C: Deploy to production and monitor for errors. Rollback if issues detected within 1 hour.
options.D: Only test schema compatibility. No data validation. Assume correct input always produces correct output.

explanation: Comprehensive ETL testing requires multiple layers: 1) Unit tests with local Spark validate transformation logic cheaply and quickly (pytest + PySpark, mocked S3). Edge case fixtures (nulls, malformed rows, extra columns for schema drift) ensure defensive handling. 2) Integration tests in dev Glue verify the job works end-to-end in the actual Glue environment with real connections. 3) Performance regression tests catch accidental inefficiencies (new code that is correct but 10x slower). 4) Data quality assertions (DQDL rules in test jobs) verify output meets quality standards. 5) All automated in CodePipeline — no deployment to production without passing all test levels. Option A (manual) is slow and error-prone. Option C (test in prod) risks customer impact. Option D (schema only) misses logic bugs.

question:
A company needs to implement a data pipeline that handles late-arriving data. Their event stream processes user actions with an event_time field. Events can arrive up to 7 days late due to mobile offline mode. The pipeline computes daily aggregates (DAU, revenue per day, conversion rates) that must be updated when late data arrives. Downstream consumers need to know when a day's aggregates have been recomputed.

Which architecture handles late-arriving data correctly?

options.A: Process events by arrival time (processing time). Ignore late arrivals. Accept inaccurate aggregates.
options.B: Kinesis Data Analytics (Flink) with event-time windowing and watermarks (watermark delay = 7 days). Late events update the appropriate day's window. Output to S3 Iceberg table (MERGE INTO updates the affected day's aggregate row). Iceberg table change notifications via S3 event notifications when files are rewritten. Downstream consumers poll or subscribe to change notifications to detect recomputed days. A 'last_computed' timestamp column in the aggregate table indicates freshness.
options.C: Batch reprocessing: Every night, recompute the last 7 days of aggregates from raw events (brute force). Always overwrites previous aggregates. No incremental updates.
options.D: Lambda consuming from Kinesis. If event_time is today, process normally. If event_time is in the past, write to a 'late arrivals' queue. Separate batch job processes late arrivals daily.

explanation: Event-time windowing with watermarks is the standard solution for late-arriving data: 1) Flink's event-time processing assigns each event to the correct daily window regardless of when it arrives. 2) Watermarks (7-day delay) keep windows open for late events and trigger recomputation when late data arrives. 3) Iceberg MERGE INTO efficiently updates only the affected day's aggregate row (not reprocessing all 7 days). 4) S3 event notifications/Iceberg change tracking notifies downstream consumers that specific days were recomputed (they know to refresh). 5) The last_computed timestamp provides transparency. Option A (ignore late data) produces inaccurate results. Option C (brute force 7-day recompute) works but wastes compute — reprocesses 7 days even if only 1 day had late arrivals. Option D (separate queue) adds complexity and delays late data processing until the next batch run.

question:
A company uses Amazon DynamoDB for their real-time analytics counters (page views, clicks, conversions). They need to: increment counters atomically at 100,000 updates per second, read current counter values with sub-millisecond latency, aggregate counters across time dimensions (hourly, daily, monthly rollups), and export counter data to their data lake for historical analysis without impacting production reads/writes.

Which DynamoDB design supports all requirements?

options.A: Single item per counter with atomic UpdateItem (ADD operation). DAX for sub-millisecond reads. DynamoDB Streams + Lambda for time-based rollups (hourly Lambda aggregates per-event counters into hourly items). DynamoDB export to S3 (no read capacity consumed) for data lake integration. Table design: PK=METRIC#page_views, SK=MINUTE#2024-01-15T10:30 for minute-level granularity.
options.B: Write every event as a separate item. Scan to count. No aggregation. Full table export nightly.
options.C: ElastiCache Redis for counters. DynamoDB for persistence. Manual sync between them.
options.D: Kinesis Data Streams for counter events. Lambda aggregating to DynamoDB every minute. DAX for reads. No export capability.

explanation: This design handles all requirements: 1) Atomic UpdateItem with ADD expression supports 100K updates/sec (DynamoDB scales writes horizontally). The partition key distributes load across the table. 2) DAX provides sub-millisecond read latency for current counter values. 3) DynamoDB Streams captures every increment — Lambda triggered by streams rolls up minute counters into hourly/daily/monthly items (separate items in the same table with different SK patterns). 4) DynamoDB Export to S3 is a zero-impact operation (reads from a point-in-time backup, not the live table) — perfect for data lake integration without affecting production. 5) The PK/SK design (METRIC#name / MINUTE#timestamp) enables efficient range queries for time-series access. Option B (scan to count) is extremely inefficient. Option C (Redis) adds a second system to manage. Option D (Kinesis → Lambda) adds ingestion latency.

question:
A company needs to implement a data classification system for their data lake. They want to automatically classify every dataset into sensitivity levels: PUBLIC (open data), INTERNAL (company confidential), SENSITIVE (PII, financial), and RESTRICTED (healthcare, legal). Classification must drive: encryption key assignment (different KMS keys per level), access control policies (Lake Formation grants per level), retention policies (different lifecycle per level), and audit requirements (enhanced logging for SENSITIVE and RESTRICTED).

Which implementation ties classification to enforcement MOST effectively?

options.A: Manual classification via spreadsheet. Security team applies policies manually based on classification. Annual re-classification review.
options.B: Amazon Macie for automated PII/sensitive data detection. Classification results written as Glue Data Catalog table properties. Lake Formation LF-Tags applied based on classification (SENSITIVITY=PUBLIC|INTERNAL|SENSITIVE|RESTRICTED). LF-Tag-based permissions automatically grant access based on user's clearance level. S3 bucket policies enforce encryption with classification-specific KMS keys. S3 lifecycle rules keyed on S3 object tags (set by Glue ETL based on LF-Tag). CloudTrail data events enabled only for SENSITIVE/RESTRICTED tagged resources (cost optimization). EventBridge rule: when Macie detects unclassified sensitive data → Lambda auto-classifies and applies LF-Tag.
options.C: Tag all S3 objects manually. IAM policies using tag conditions. No automated detection.
options.D: Store all data with the same encryption and access controls (treat everything as RESTRICTED). Simplifies management but over-restricts access to public/internal data.

explanation: This creates an automated classification-to-enforcement pipeline: 1) Macie automatically detects sensitive content (no manual classification for PII). 2) LF-Tags as the classification mechanism integrate natively with Lake Formation permissions (access is automatically scoped by tag). 3) Tag-based encryption ensures each sensitivity level uses appropriate key strength. 4) Lifecycle rules by tag implement retention policies automatically. 5) Selective CloudTrail data events save cost (only enhanced logging where needed). 6) EventBridge automation ensures new unclassified data gets properly classified without human intervention. The key insight: LF-Tags serve as the single classification mechanism driving all downstream policies (access, encryption, retention, audit) — change the tag, all policies update automatically. Option A (manual) doesn't scale. Option C (manual tags) lacks automation. Option D (over-classify) restricts legitimate access.

question:
A company needs to build a real-time ETL pipeline that transforms and loads data into Amazon Redshift with sub-minute latency. Source data comes from multiple microservices publishing events to Amazon MSK. The events need to be: deserialized (Avro format with Schema Registry), joined with reference data (product catalog in DynamoDB), transformed (flatten nested structures, compute derived fields), and loaded into Redshift for real-time dashboards.

Which pipeline architecture achieves sub-minute latency to Redshift?

options.A: MSK → Kinesis Data Firehose → S3 → Redshift COPY (every 5 minutes). Separate Lambda for transformation.
options.B: MSK → Flink (Kinesis Data Analytics) for deserialization, DynamoDB enrichment (AsyncIO), and transformation → Amazon Redshift streaming ingestion (materialized view on Kinesis Data Stream as intermediate). Flink outputs to a Kinesis Data Stream which Redshift consumes via streaming ingestion materialized view.
options.C: MSK → Lambda (event source mapping) → Redshift Data API (INSERT statements). One insert per event.
options.D: MSK → Spark Structured Streaming on EMR → JDBC write to Redshift (micro-batch every 30 seconds).

explanation: This architecture achieves sub-minute end-to-end latency: 1) Flink consumes from MSK with Avro deserialization and Schema Registry integration (built-in). 2) Flink's AsyncIO performs non-blocking DynamoDB lookups for enrichment. 3) Flink applies transformations (flatMap, computed fields) in-stream. 4) Flink outputs to Kinesis Data Streams. 5) Redshift streaming ingestion reads from Kinesis via a materialized view — data is queryable in Redshift within seconds of arriving in Kinesis. This is the fastest path to queryable data in Redshift. Option A (Firehose → S3 → COPY) has 5+ minute latency. Option C (per-event INSERT) doesn't scale and is extremely inefficient for Redshift (designed for bulk loads). Option D (Spark JDBC) is slower than streaming ingestion and JDBC writes are suboptimal for Redshift.

question:
A company's data platform serves 10 downstream teams. They need to implement a data contract system where: data producers define and publish contracts (schema, SLAs, quality guarantees), consumers can discover and subscribe to contracts, breaking changes (schema modifications that would affect consumers) are detected before deployment, and contract violations (SLA breaches, quality drops) trigger alerts to both producers and affected consumers.

Which implementation provides a complete data contract system?

options.A: Documentation in Confluence. Manual communication between teams about changes. Hope for the best.
options.B: AWS Glue Schema Registry for contract schema definition (Avro/JSON schemas with compatibility modes). BACKWARD compatibility mode prevents breaking changes at schema level (producers can't publish incompatible schemas). DataZone for contract publication and consumer subscription (producers publish data products with SLA metadata, consumers subscribe). Glue Data Quality for contract quality enforcement (DQDL rules embedded in producer pipelines — violations trigger EventBridge events). CloudWatch metrics for SLA monitoring (latency, freshness). EventBridge routing: contract violation events → SNS → both producer and consumer teams notified. CI/CD pipeline validates schema compatibility before deployment (Schema Registry compatibility check in CodePipeline).
options.C: Custom REST API for contract management. Teams register contracts manually. Database stores contract definitions. Periodic checks for compliance.
options.D: Apache Avro schemas in Git. Pull request reviews for changes. No runtime enforcement.

explanation: This provides a complete data contract lifecycle: 1) Schema Registry defines the technical contract (schema) with compatibility enforcement — BACKWARD mode means consumers reading with old schema can always read new messages (prevents breaking changes at serialization level). 2) CI/CD compatibility check in CodePipeline catches breaking changes before they reach production. 3) DataZone provides the discovery/subscription layer — consumers formally subscribe and producers understand their consumer base. 4) Glue Data Quality enforces quality guarantees at runtime. 5) CloudWatch tracks SLA compliance. 6) EventBridge + SNS notifies both parties on violations (producer to fix, consumer to be aware). Option A (documentation) has no enforcement. Option C (custom API) requires building everything from scratch. Option D (Git schemas) catches changes in review but has no runtime enforcement.
